data.title: AMD Q1: You Still Have Time To Buy Before The Sentiment Shift
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD), the stock has gained 5% in price.

AMD reported excellent Q1 2025 results, with 36% year-over-year growth from revenues of $7.44 billion. The standout in the quarter, though, was the Data Center segment, with robust demand for EPYC server processors and Instinct GPUs. Growth such as this is not surprising given the tailwinds from the emerging AI revolution.

Non-GAAP earnings rose 55% to $1.57 billion. This is evidence of good operational leverage with scaling revenues. Despite all of this momentum, there are certainly significant near-term headwinds for AMD. Most prominently, U.S. export controls, which management estimates may negatively impact 2025 revenues by approximately $1.5 billion.

However, as an optimistic assumption, a 12-month price target of $200 does not seem impossible to me. This would result from profit margin expansion, additional market share gains, and macro tailwinds, tempered by China pressures.

Q1 Analysis

AMD's Q1 performance was led by the Data Center segment, with spectacular year-over-year revenues of 57% to around $3.67 billion. Alliances with industry giants, like Meta (META) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), affirm AMD's increasing role in providing infrastructure for the AI revolution. The Client segment also improved, rising year-over-year by 68% to $2.3 billion. This was due to high demand for the new Zen 5 Ryzen processors. However, the Gaming segment contracted, with revenues declining year-over-year by 30% due to softening semi-custom demand as the console generation matures.

Gross margins rose to 50% on a GAAP basis, from 47% a year ago. This reflects data center products with higher margins. Operating margins expanded to 24% on a non-GAAP basis, reflecting cost discipline and scale in operations.

There was a relatively modest year-over-year revenue decline of just 3%, to $823 million, for AMD's Embedded segment. However, this segment still provides diversification and stable margins.

AMD's acquisition of ZT Systems further contributes to AMD's vertical integration, enabling the company to more effectively monetize a meaningful share of its TAM in AI infrastructure products; the market could be $500 billion in size by 2028, according to some current dominant estimates. I think it will be higher as macroeconomic conditions improve and the progress of AI becomes much more obvious socio-operationally.

Despite its strengths, the company does face headwinds from regulatory risks. Most notably, export controls on advanced AI chips. The company has already outlined an $800 million inventory charge for Q2 2025.

Valuation & Macro Analysis

AMD stock could reach $200 in a bull-case outcome in 12 months' time. However, more conservatively, we are looking at about $175 in a base case. In this analysis, I will run you through the bull-case outcome. I am growing more optimistic about positive macroeconomic outcomes, which could cause very strong stock market sentiment amid strong growth in 2026.

I anticipate AMD achieving a TTM non-GAAP net margin of about 30% in mid-2026, driven by continued favorable product mix shifts and operational leverage from increased data center revenue. With 1.65 billion shares outstanding and projected TTM revenue of about $37 billion in mid-2026, AMD would generate approximately $11 billion in non-GAAP net income and non-GAAP EPS of $6.75.

Applying a non-GAAP P/E multiple of 30 is justified by the company's robust revenue growth, expanding margins, and strategic position within the high-growth AI and data center markets. This results in a 12-month price target of $200, as specified earlier.

Technically speaking, we are still experiencing downward pressure on the stock price. However, to get the best returns in the market, you have to pre-empt the inflection point. I believe it has already happened, but you haven't missed the major bull run that's coming if you buy in now. If you look closely at the following chart, you can see the start of recovery in April. This upward momentum has been building for about a month now. I find it very unlikely that further downward pressure will resume given favorable macro indicators, like the major U.S.-UK trade deal setting the stage for further international deals to come. These deals will remove non-tariff barriers and set the stage for a bull market in 2026 based on deregulation and tax tailwinds, as well as a reformed free-trade horizon. I wrote about this recently in an Editor's Pick I achieved on Seeking Alpha, and it is nice to see my thesis coming to fruition.

Risk Analysis

I consider AMD to be a low-risk bet on AI infrastructure, but several risks could impact my price target. Regulatory and geopolitical challenges remain prominent regarding U.S. semiconductor export controls. The $800 million inventory write-down expected in Q2 (due to export restrictions to China for advanced AI chips) underscores the materiality of this risk. That said, the Trump administration may potentially ease these export restrictions, which were implemented under Biden. The Biden-era "AI Diffusion Rule" will be revoked, but I expect that as AMD and Nvidia (NVDA) keep pressing the administration for continued global chip influence, China will be allowed to trade with the U.S. under careful U.S. oversight.

There is also the overbearing risk of AMD's reliance on TSMC (TSM). It will probably be a few years before TSMC is operational at scale in Arizona, so for the time being, the risk of a Chinese invasion of Taiwan looms. The quicker the newly outlined TSMC Arizona plants are active, the better. I'm still holding about 17.5-20% in cash due to the Taiwan invasion risk. I'd like to deploy some more of that cash (potentially down to about 12.5-15%), but at the moment it still seems too risky.

The Achilles heel of Western capitalism really is Taiwan right now. We should just be thankful that a Chinese invasion would be the equivalent of a bee sting: China would self-destruct its long-term reputation and near-term economics on impact, while the U.S.-led world order would most likely survive (with gained bolstered international reputation and the associated long-term geo-economic security).

Conclusion: Strong Buy

AMD's robust Q1 2025 performance clarifies its position in the emerging AI revolution. Despite China-related regulatory headwinds facing AMD, value-generating deals and relentless innovation still put it squarely in secular growth markets. With realistic macroeconomic assumptions and sensible multiples, my model comes out with an enticing 12-month target of $200 per share in a bull-case outcome. This valuation reflects anticipated margin improvements, ongoing market share gains, and macroeconomic stability.

That said, investors must carefully monitor key risks, particularly geopolitical developments and competitive dynamics. I'm an AMD shareholder, and I hold it at close to 10% of my portfolio right now. I believe it will be one of the most rewarding and least-risky stocks to capitalize on strong AI market sentiment in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Palantir: It's Just Too Expensive Here (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I have been bullish on Palantir Technologies Inc.'s (NASDAQ:PLTR) stock for several years now. In fact, I built this badly beaten-down stock and highly misunderstood company into my most significant holding in late 2022 and early 2023, when its share price was only around $7.

Palantir - Double Top Pattern

To say that Palantir has done well is a massive understatement. This stock has been on fire, appreciating by a mind-bending 2,000% (peak-to-trough) in only about two years. Palantir was also the best performing stock in in the S&P 500 (SP500) in 2024.

Of course, I decreased my Palantir position along the way. What was once a 10-15% portfolio holding is now only about 2% despite the enormous price appreciation.

Now, I still love the company because it's dominant in its field and should continue to be very successful. Nonetheless, the primary reason why I decreased much of my Palantir position around the $80-100 level was because the valuation became hyper-elevated.

This dynamic made the prospects of making more considerable near-term highs very unlikely. Also, technically, the stock had become staggeringly overbought above $100 in early February.

We witnessed a massive "correction" leading up into and after April 2. The stock dropped from about $125 to approximately $65, roughly a 48% decline in only about five weeks.

The Report Was Good, But Not Out Of This World Good

Palantir reported earnings recently, and while the results were solid, they were not stellar, in my view. Yes, most of the key metrics were better than expected, and the guidance was strong, but we did not witness a "Nvidia Moment."

Palantir reported Q1 EPS of 13 cents (Non-GAAP), which was inline with estimates. The company also provided 8 cents in GAAP EPS, beating the consensus estimate by one penny. Revenue came in at about $884M, nearly a 40% YoY increase.

While the sales guidance was higher, it was about 4% above the consensus estimates, which is not 40%. Also, some analysts are expressing international growth concerns following the quarter. I would add that the stock's valuation has swelled so obscenely that it may need more significant growth to justify further increases.

Let's Remain Rational

Palantir now has a market cap of about $280 billion. I remember when $100 billion seemed insane. Why? Because Palantir had only about $2.87 billion in sales last year, and could achieve about $3.9B in sales this year, and roughly $5B in revenues next year.

So, we are dealing with a stock that trades close to 100 times TTM sales, and even if we value Palantir relative to next year's sales projections, its valuation is around 55-60 times 2026 expected sales.

This valuation is uber-expensive, and if we look at Palantir from a P/E basis, it's still extremely costly here.

Palantir could earn about $0.58 this year, and roughly $0.73 next year. This dynamic illustrates that Palantir's forward P/E ratio is over 200, and its 2026 P/E ratio is around 160. In a more bullish case outcome, I believe Palantir could deliver about $0.80 in EPS next year.

Yet, this still puts Palantir's forward P/E ratio at about 150, extremely elevated, and likely limiting near term upside potential in Palantir's stock. This dynamic also makes the stock more vulnerable to a considerable correction as the ultra high valuation could lead to increased selling pressure as we advance.

When To Buy The Dip In Palantir

There are some clear dip buying opportunities in Palantir, but they must be substantial dips, in my view. We witnessed a solid opportunity on April 7th, as the swift drop to $65 created a very oversold environment for Palantir.

Unfortunately, Palantir is the poster child for a super high multiple stock. Therefore, we may see a more considerable pullback process with Palantir in the near and potentially in the intermediate term, as the market digests Palantir's valuation.

PLTR 1-Year Chart

In a bullish case scenario, Palantir could have only a mild pullback to about $100-90/50-day MA support. However, this pullback would not reset its valuation by much, and there may be limited catalysts to push this stock notably above $100 in the near term.

Therefore, Palantir could potentially decline to its previous support around $65-70, which may coincide with its 200-day MA. However, in a "worst-case" scenario in which the market may want to revalue Palantir more aggressively, we could potentially witness a slide down to the critical $50-40 level support.

I know, it's a long way down, but I've seen much stranger things occur. Therefore, and despite its remarkable potential and dominant market-leading position, it would not surprise me to see Palantir decline to about $50 in the next general market pullback.

Also, at about 50 bucks per share, Palantir would trade at a much more attractive 75 forward (2026) P/E multiple. While it's not cheap, it's better than the current 160 forward P/E.

Also, Palantir could outperform EPS estimates. Therefore, we may see EPS coming in at $0.80-0.90 next year. Now, if Palantir's stock closed the long-standing gap around $40, its forward P/E would be about 45-50 here, which seems like a highly reasonable buy-in valuation for Palantir.

Even around $50, Palantir's valuation of 50-60 on a forward P/E basis becomes very attractive in my view. Palantir could increase EPS YoY from about $0.60-0.80 (33%) in 2026.

Given that its forward P/E could be about 55 at $50, Palantir's PEG ratio would be a much more reasonable 1.66. Currently, Palantir's forward (2026) PEG ratio is at about 4.5, which is very high, and is another reason for its current hold rating.

The Silver Lining

Palantir's stock could continue doing well in the intermediate and long term. I don't want to be negative on Palantir. It's an excellent American company, and it has a remarkable business with enormous potential. Nonetheless, there is such a thing as being too expensive, and the market may need to reevaluate Palantir in the near-term. Still, Palantir has considerable potential in the intermediate and long-term.

Where Palantir's stock could go in future years

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Revenue (in billions) $3.9 $5.25 $6.8 $8.6 $10.9 $13.6
Revenue growth 34% 35% 29% 27% 26% 25%
EPS $0.61 $0.85 $1.14 $1.48 $1.90 $2.40
EPS growth 49% 39% 34% 30% 29% 25%
Forward P/E 117 105 95 90 80 75
Stock price $100 $120 $140 $170 $192 $205

Source: The Financial Prophet.

I've reduced some of my price and valuation targets since my previous Palantir analysis. Still, I believe Palantir could achieve better than anticipated results in a more bullish case outcome. This dynamic could enable Palantir's stock to appreciate more considerably and more rapidly than anticipated. Nonetheless, we would need to see more considerable growth developments at Palantir, in my view. For now, I've reduced my rating from a buy to a hold and I've adjusted my 2025 price target from $120 to $100.

data.title: One Ring To Rule Them All: The Price Of Oil
data.description:
The relatively rapid decline in oil prices during the past the last months is leading to a different outlook for U.S. oil and gas. Globally, to 2024, we had been producing 22% of the crude and liquids market and 26% of natural gas. That’s a huge supply-side accomplishment.

The pricing impact of shale has been enormous. The Dallas Fed found that oil prices in 2018 would have been around 36% higher if the shale revolution did not take place. Further, the shale revolution implies a reduction in current oil price volatility around 25% and a decline in long-run volatility of more than 50%. Kiss that goodbye. Considering that the price of oil dictates so much around the world, and geopolitics are a key source of volatility, the world may look different.

While the industry has faced such macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds with aplomb in the past, the geological and economic issues surrounding peak oil are different. I’ll attempt a brief analysis that shows why it’s important. Price discovery of other goods is tied to the price of oil, too.

This research note will analyze the statements recently made by CEO Vicki Hollub of Occidental Petroleum (OXY) and Diamondback (FANG) CEO Travis Stice. I will also corroborate it with other insights and analysis. Hollub says U.S. shale may peak in 2027, but moved it up to 2026 potentially, owing to the volatile macro environment. The spot price of WTI was $73 Jan. 31 and $60 May 2, lowering price to a new marker.

Tipping points

Diamondback CEO Travis Stice said:

"We believe we are at a tipping point for U.S. oil production at current commodity prices. (The) U.S. frac crew count is already down ~15% this year, with the Permian Basin crew count down ~20% from its January peak, and expected to decline further. U.S. oil directed rig counts (are) to be down almost 10% by the end of the second quarter and decline further in the third quarter. (Thus) it is likely that U.S. onshore oil production has peaked and will begin to decline this quarter."

From the shareholder letter of May 5, Stice continues:

“Over the past decade, the cost of supply for the average barrel of oil produced in the United States has increased. The shale revolution has evolved from proof of concept (outspend cash flow to prove up basins) to manufacturing mode (significant growth) and is now in a more mature stage of development (free cash flow generation and return of capital).”

He's acknowledging the recent tariff policy moves that have reduced the price of crude and are now coming home to roost. OPEC, mainly through Saudi Arabia’s leadership, will find its advantage, attempting to reduce shale’s once-growing market share. This call on OPEC was thought to be happening in the 2030s from forecasts five years back, pre-pandemic.

"Geologic headwinds outweighing tailwinds" provided by improvements in technology and operational efficiency were also highlighted by Stice. In a feature of mine from last year, this same idea is presented slightly differently with a similar meaning. Noted by a shale pioneer just one year ago, "At some point the pace of tech advances may slow, that is, the laws of diminishing returns." Last year, global peak oil projections ranged from 106 m/b/d to 116 per IEA and OPEC, respectively. It was thought to go higher yet by transforming globally abundant gas to liquids even. U.S. production was hovering around 13 m/b/d, and 14 million could be seen, “but 15-16 is a stretch,” according to the pioneering executive.

Merge and move forward

More M&A activity might result because of the pricing environment. Consolidations have occurred as long as the industry's been around, notes the executive. Asked about this time, he says, “I think it's different in degrees, though not completely. Consolidations are typically the product of the majors or large companies using one of their big strategic advantages, which is capital.” But the rebuilding of inventory as before, in the wave that set off the first shale revolutions in the 2000-2010s when the independents acquired acreage left behind by the majors, may be different.

There is an emphasis on the next wave of opportunities likely being international, he notes, where shale tech and advancements can be applied for market needs. We don't know what the technology is going to be tomorrow that will create another opportunity,” the pioneer offers. I've seen M&A cycle through with an important effect on the structure of the industry.” For example, large amounts of capital were required to achieve scale in U.S. unconventional resource development. Source: "The Long Runway for New Energy Demand"

The idea "hydrocarbons are still a growth business" from last year is still true, to degrees now, for those that can leverage advantages. They exist. In 2018, BP bought into the Permian:

“This (acquisition) transforms our Lower 48 business and reinforces our position as a top onshore producer in the United States. It adds assets which today produce around 190,000 boe/p/d across 470,000 acres, with around 4.6 billion barrels of discovered resource.”

This was a $10.5 billion purchase of BHP’s assets in Texas, in fact from early successes in the Eagle Ford shale. Today, the $73 billion-cap BP is being consider for acquisition by Shell ( SHEL), Chevron ( CVX), Exxon Mobil ( XOM), TotalEnergies ( TTE) and Abu Dhabi's Adnoc as they analyze the potential, says Seeking Alpha from sources.

M&A was slowing from the big wave occurring from 2021 to 2024. But now, more is evident in the oil and gas patch. At a February NAPE conference in Houston, a top private equity leader noted other successes regarding shale’s overall economic impact:

  • WTI prices: 24% decrease ($100 to $76 per barrel)
  • Natural gas: 73% decrease
  • Utility gas: 2% decrease

On peaking and impact

If U.S. shale peaks in the 13 million b/d range, and declines, associated gas production also declines. Associated gas comprises roughly 25-30% of produced gas. The peak of U.S. oil was predicted in the 2027-2030 range by Hollub at a conference just two weeks ago. This shows you the effect of the deteriorated environment that executives now have to navigate, especially around first quarter earnings.

Further, I parsed research on peaking of resources in 2010, with the following takeaways that are relevant now:

“Any well-functioning market economy, endowed with a limited amount of an exhaustible resource, will apportion production through time according to prevailing economic incentives that reflect market fundamentals, by which I mean the cost of production, discount rates, the strength of current versus future demand, and the availability of substitutes.” —James Smith, energy economist, formerly of SMU Cox School of Business.

In a market-driven economy, this is how it works. “Producers are driven by attempts to maximize profits,” Smith explains. “As a producer, I have oil reserves. If I believe they will be really scarce in five years I’m not going to produce as much today because I’ll be rewarded more later when price is higher. So I leave these resources in the ground. That’s part of an inter-temporal balancing.”

This is exactly what is happening. What does it mean? Production will slow as the large gas players had to do in 2023-2024, owing to low prices, and now with oil-weighted firms doing the same. Oil prices will ultimately rise to meet the economics of production and shareholder expectations of free cash flow-induced dividends. I’ve seen modeling of what types of acreage are drilled at $90 WTI versus $70. Lower oil prices dictate less drilling of remaining core acreages in key basins.

Additionally, economics will also rule. Sticky prices and production, ie., supply and demand inelasticities, will reveal the true prices we are to pay. Stagflation could become visible. The charts are changing.

The price of oil is a vital economic proxy for the health of the global economic order, the one price to rule them all. This has been a driver of the last decades. Interesting is what comes next. The oil patch has invariably received a shock, from a more moderate trajectory of the past. The very volatility that shale helped curtail in oil markets is back.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Time Is Different
data.description:
"Recession Warnings Are Everywhere, Except in the Data."

So read the headlines of the article by Ben Casselman and Colby Smith in the New York Times.

"Evidence for the economic impact of President Trump's trade wars is everywhere--except, for the most part, in economic data itself."

"Consumer spending hasn't fallen. Layoffs haven't risen. Businesses haven't stopped investing in equipment or buying supplies.

"Economists say it is a matter of time before the impact of tariffs and the uncertainty that Trump's on-again, off-again approach to trade policy has created begin to show up in the hard data."

"But, until then, they are left sifting through crumbs of evidence that wouldn't get a second glance in more normal times: customs revenue, hotel bookings in Las Vegas, freight shipments by truck and rail."

"The problem is we don't have much to hang onto at this point," said Marc Giannoni, chief U.S. economist for Barclays.

"We have to rely on anecdotes, on indicators that are nonconventional."

This time is different!

One should smile after this last sentence.

However, I would argue that this time is different.

I would argue that investors need to keep their eyes on the banking system.

I would argue that the economy has lots and lots of money "hanging around." Money that can be drawn on and used to support "normal" spending patterns.

The money is there...the economic system just needs to draw on it.

In fact, I have been writing about the presence of these monies in the commercial banking system for a long time.

My question is...is this the time that these monies are going to be used? Is this the time that the "excess reserves" in the banking system will be drawn on?

Is this time different?

Let's look at the commercial banking system.

These "Reserve Balances" with Federal Reserve Banks represent the cash balances that commercial banks hold either within their own vaults, with other banks, and with the Federal Reserve.

Notice: something happened in 2008.

Here we find that Ben Bernanke was at work. As I have discussed in many different times and in many different places, Mr. Bernanke, as Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, changed how the Federal Reserve functioned when it came to the conduct of monetary policy.

Many changes took place within the commercial banking system, the financial markets, and the Federal Reserve System at this time. We are living with the consequences of these changes.

From 2008 through to 2020, the banking system lived through three rounds of quantitative easing, a time when the Fed increased its holdings of government securities in a regularly, pre-planned approach. As can be seen, the "Reserve Balances" or excess reserves in the commercial banking system increased as this quantitative easing took place.

Reserve Balances before 2008 varied in a range from $5 billion to $35 billion.

Reserve Balances first hit $1 trillion around 2010 and remained above this total since then.

In 2020, the Covid-19 pandemic hit and the Federal Reserve moved into "fast gear" in order to protect the American financial system from collapsing.

By 2020, reserve balancers exceeded $4 trillion.

In March 2020, the Federal Reserve began to reduce the size of its securities portfolio, going into a mode of quantitative tightening to reverse the injection of money into the commercial banking system that it had been doing before.

Since March 2020, the Federal Reserve has continued to engage in quantitative tightening, to where reserve balances now stand at more than $3.2 trillion in the most recent Federal Reserve statistics.

This number is very close to the line item "Cash Assets" found on the Federal Reserve statistical release H.8, "Assets and Liabilities of Commercial Banks in the United States." In the banking week ending April 23, commercial banks in the U.S. held around $3.6 trillion in "Cash Assets."

The point I have been trying to make in my recent writing on this topic is that commercial banks, in spite of all the rules and regulations that work around the cash holdings of these organizations, the commercial banking system has a lot of excess reserves hanging around.

The commercial banking system is in good shape and has the "cash" on hand to support a fairly substantial "burst" in liquidity in the financial system if the right situation arises.

That is, the Federal Reserve would not have to go through a period where it generated a lot of bank stimulus to get the banking system lending, to keep the economy spending, and to avoid a recession at this time.

This is why "Consumer spending hasn't fallen. Layoffs haven't risen. Businesses haven't stopped investing in equipment or buying supplies."

We don't have sufficient proof yet to really say that this is really what is happening.

But, it seems to me that it could be an adequate argument for the result.

If so, it would make "this time different!"

So, I will argue that investors should watch the evolution taking place in the economy and consider this argument as a reason for what we are seeing.

As Mr. Giannoni suggested, as cited above, "The problem is we don't have much to hang onto at this point."

We must look elsewhere in order to understand.

But, we have never had so much money "pumped" into the economy as we have had "pumped" in to fight Covid-19.

There is no question that the commercial banking system has accumulated a lot of "excess cash."

There is no question that the commercial banking system has, up to this point in time, sat on the cash and not put it out to use.

But, the time may have arrived that the banking system feels OK in expanding the use of these funds.

As a consequence, this time could be different.

data.title: Swatch Group: The Opportunity I'm Seeing
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I will be establishing my basic and initial thesis on Swatch Group AG (OTCPK:SWGAY). Swatch Group is a Swiss-based holding company that works in, you guessed it, watches. If you follow my articles on luxury companies, you'll know that good watches are actually one of my personal weaknesses. I own a few from several premier brands, and this article will cover a company that has some of those brands within its portfolio.

Swatch Group doesn't have the best yield, but that's "in style" for Swiss companies; they tend not to have high yields, especially in the consumer sector. However, they have other qualities that make them attractive at the right valuation.

And I will argue and show you that the Swatch Group is actually seeing enough valuation decline to justify the company's rating of "BUY", which is the rating that I'm going to give the company here.

That being said, Swatch Group is not in a position where it's somehow the "greatest company to invest in" at this time. It's in a position where I can, after some time, see an upside, which is good enough. But a market-leading investment?

We have some other companies to look at for that, and I'll show you why, even when it comes to this sector, Swatch is more like competing for the position of being a good buy on a peer basis.

Of course, if you somehow agree with the market's premiumization of the company here, then there is a way to see a market-beating annualized RoR - and that's what I'll also show you today.

So, let's see what we've got here.

Swatch Group - Some of the best watches in the world

I've covered a few premium Swiss companies in a few sectors so far. This is the company that owns a few of the most premium watch brands in the entire world. The list here includes brands like Omega, Glashütte, Blancpain, Breguet, Mido, Longines, Tissot, Rado, and Harry Winston. I would say that out of this list, Omega and Blancpain are some of the more "premium" brands, with the others being more consumer-grade mechanical and other types of watches. Not that these are bad in any way - but they don't have the same quality or appeal, or perception of appeal (depending on your view) of Rolex, Vacheron, Audemars Piguet, or others.

The company originated over 90 years ago, when Omega merged with watchmaker Tissot. The company advanced further from here on, and merged in the early 1980s with the help of a private investor very knowledgeable about Swiss banking and private investment. It was renamed to the Swatch Group in 1986.

Furthermore, the company owns the subsidiary ETA S.A., which is one of the world's largest manufacturers of watch movements. It also owns another Swiss company that I have covered in the past, Richemont (OTCPK:CFRUY), which markets some of the best watch brands in the entire world, including brands like IWC, Panerai, Vacheron, and others.

The company also operates a watchmaking school with offices across the world. Based on this, it could easily be argued that the company is a vertically integrated watch company with a global set of operations.

The company has annual sales in excess of 6.7B CHF (2024). The latest trends, which we see in the valuation for the company, are a negative one with a double-digit top-line decline. That being said, the company typically manages a double-digit 10 %+ operating margin, which improved in the 2024 period, with a net income margin of between 10-11%, generating almost 800-900M CHF typically, down to 220M CHF in the 2024 period.

The company also, after 2024, cut its dividend by about 20% since the last fiscal report.

The overall trend for the Swatch group was a general decline in sales and results, resulting from very troubled macroeconomic and sector-specific pressure. Orders were down - in fact, most things were down. It's also why the company CEO in her annual message to shareholders mostly spoke about the pride for the Swatch group for producing/manufacturing locally in Switzerland, for their watchmaking schools, and their employment of people. The company also used wording like "these are not gigantic figures", expressing clearly how bad 2024 was for the business (Source).

As of 2024, the company employs over 32,000 people, has 16 watch brands/jewelry brands, 2 retail brands, 30+ production companies, over 150 production sites in expensive Switzerland, and over 30 countries with the Swatch Group organization.

This sort of operational overhead means that any sort of downturn in the market, like the one we're seeing here, has the very real potential of turning earnings not only close to negative, but negative. Earnings for the year on a per share basis are down to 3.74 CHF/share for 2024, and back in 2020 during COVID, earnings actually went negative for the fiscal. (Source: F.A.S.T Graphs paywalled link)

The thing about Swatch Group is that its sector and expertise seem to ensure that it trades at a premium. However, what I am seeing is that the premium may not often be justified, and we're better off forecasting it at a far, far lower multiple. The company as an investment has actually not been a "great" sort of return. Looking at the 20-year returns, it's a significant underperformance of the market.

What this tells me is that valuation is crucial, and that this company shouldn't necessarily be your long-term investment choice unless something drastically changes. You'll also note that today's annual EPS is actually unchanged or close to unchanged to that we saw 20 years ago.

And the negatives in the fundamentals don't stop there. The company, despite what you see above, isn't all that forecastable either. We have a 10-year negative forecast accuracy of over 70% of the time, with a 10-20% margin of error, which makes this easily one of the least forecastable Swiss luxury companies that I have covered in my time here on Seeking Alpha. Not exactly flattering, compared to some of the other companies I have looked at here. Looking at the market share for the company's products, it's also clear that Swatch has struggled with its growth numbers since the introduction of the digital watches from Apple (AAPL), and a decline in Asian consumer segments. This is no surprise given the company's focus on some brands that I would consider more commoditized than other Swiss brands, and luxury brands. A Tissot or some of their budget-friendly brands is more easily replaceable for its consumers than a Rolex is for a Rolex consumer/owner.

These are some of the primary risks I see for Swatch.

But despite the risks mentioned, this company has upside, especially at a good valuation.

The Swatch group should not be underestimated, especially now that it's cheaper than it has been since around the time of COVID-19. While I would have been neutral on the company at any time over the last 3-4 years, up until a few months ago, that is no longer the case.

Swatch group trades in a peer group of very few, because most of the good watch companies are not actually publicly traded. So to have one like Swatch Group at a cheap or competitive price, in a sector that's usually not available for investment, that justifies paying attention.

Aside from the valuation upside over time, there's the very clear potential of recovery as a major potential benefit. The company's current 2025E outlook is forecasting exactly this reversal, though the exact degree or size of it may be in question. Given the company's failure to reach forecasts, we should also moderate this somewhat in terms of EPS - but the direction is, I believe, correct, with the exception of China, where the market is expected to be muted in 2025E as well (Source). However, in total, the company is expecting a very significant reversal in cash flow, earnings, and sales.

This is based on record sales as well as good market share gains in core markets, which include US, Japan, India, EMEA and parts of Asia excluding China, with strongest growth in the company's core brands, which include Omega, Tissot and Longines, which are sort of what Swatch is "known for". Translating this market share gain for sales in 2025-2027, this would imply a non-trivial upside, and the first signs of this, in the form of reported December/early 2025 sales outside of China, are already here (Source). This, coupled with new planned products (some mentioned and shown above), promises upside here. While the upside is somewhat unclear in terms of exact RoR and sales, I believe the direction is clear enough.

In light of this, we can establish a valuation for the company as of May 2025.

Valuation for Swatch Group - Targeting below 18-20x, as opposed to above 30x.

A company that has a relatively poor long-term rate of return can be tricky to value. You definitely want to get into it at a low multiple and forecast it to a lower multiple, ignoring or at least discounting some of the premium in there. So too with this company. The 20-year average is close to 20x, and the company has at times traded above 30x.

I would target a low 5-year to 10-year average of 18x as a fair value target.

For Swatch, I expect step-by-step recovery every year in 2025-2027 or 2028 as well, with around 10-20% per year, and a strong 50% recovery in 2025, due to the reversal that we're seeing and the company is forecasting. The yield we saw in 2024 is not something I believe we'll see again for the company, even in 2027 though I believe the company will keep this low.

At a lowball target of 18x P/E, the company's implied fair value is around 190 CHF, meaning significantly below its historical highs, which at times have been as high as 400-600 CHF. Perhaps if company earnings recover to levels allowing for this, or continue to imply growth, we can get back to that - for now, I'm forecasting merely a recovery to a low multiple such as this.

This would imply an annualized RoR of 15-16% per year.

As you can see, it clears my overall hurdle of investment - it's good enough, it's a Swiss company with solid fundamentals, and it has a very attractive portfolio in its own right. Based on this, Swatch Group AG is currently a "BUY" with a fair value target range of between 190-195 CHF, which gives a market-beating upside of 15% or above as long as you buy it below 145 CHF, which also turns out to be my share price target.

For the peer perspective, I believe only one publicly traded company can be looked at - perhaps two. Richemont (OTCPK:CFRUY)(OTCPK:CFRHF) and LVMH (OTCPK:LVMUY), though the latter's portfolio in terms of watches is rather modest. Richemont is the main competition in the public space, and frankly, I believe it to be the fundamentally better company due to the quality of the brands that it represents. What's more, Richemont is more stable, A+ rated, and while the yield is less than half of this company's, it's still a solid investment in its own right, even as I am writing this.

Other analysts tend to agree with the caution about the company. Out of a full 23 analysts covering the company, only 2 have the company at a "BUY" at this time, with most at "Hold" or a negative rating. This reflects the current drop in 2024 and the uncertainty going forward. I choose to put the company at a low PT to see the upside I am seeing here (Source: Paywalled TIKR.com Link).

I would go so far as to say that, given the two in comparison, I would pick Richemont over Swatch - But if you, like me, already have Richemont, then Swatch could be a compelling diversification with an upside. But if you asked me to choose, I've made my choice here.

UHR is the native ticker for Swatch - in German, the word "Uhr" means watch, which is a fun detail. The ADR for Swatch is SWGAY, a 0.05x share ADR, meaning my target of 145 CHF comes to $8.7/share for the ADR of SWGAY.

The watch sector/luxury sector requires your definite patience for investing - that much is very clear if you look at the history of the companies in it. Based on this, my targets and thesis for the company are as follows.

Thesis

  • To call Swatch Group "Cheap" here would be a step too far. Swatch Group is an attractive watchmaking business, but with brands that are less premium and interesting than the brands represented by one of its only realistic or relevant peer portfolios, Richemont. Swatch has good fundamentals, good operations, and has one of the most European-centric production of watches in the entire world.
  • However, challenging manufacturing conditions and macro conditions have led to the company's recent decline in valuation, which ultimately have made the company more interesting as an investment. The company has a yield of over 3%, and at under 18- 20x P/E makes for a compelling entry for the long term, or at least until we see a reversal.
  • I would give the Swatch group a PT of at the very least 145 CHF with a fair value price of around 190-195 CHF, which makes this company a "BUY" towards the 2027-2028E with over 15% annualized. I would however, consider other Swiss luxury-type companies equally or more attractive here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.

  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.

  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.

  • This company is currently cheap.

  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of 145 CHF/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of May of 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Introducing The S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices
data.description:
Market participants are continuously seeking innovative tools to better understand market dynamics and manage risk. This week marks the launch of the S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices. These indices are designed to measure the historical dispersion of The 500 over various time periods, providing valuable insights into market volatility and the performance of individual stocks relative to the broader market. They also serve as an analytical complement to the Cboe S&P 500 Dispersion Index (DSPX), which measures implied dispersion over the next 30 days.

Understanding Dispersion and Its Importance

Dispersion is an important statistical measure that quantifies the range of outcomes among the components of an index during a specific period. By evaluating how individual stocks perform in relation to the overall index, it’s possible to gain insights into individual patterns and underlying risks.

The S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices offer two primary measures: the S&P 500 1-Day Realized Dispersion Index, which reflects historical dispersion over a single trading day; and the S&P 500 30-Day Realized Dispersion Index, which provides insights over a rolling 30-calendar day period. The full universe is used for the S&P 500 1-Day Realized Dispersion Index, while the 30-day version is limited to stocks that remain in the index for the entire rolling period and maintain non-zero weights; corporate actions like spin-offs are excluded to avoid distortions. These indices utilize daily and monthly returns, as well as adjusted close weights of each S&P 500 constituent, alongside the daily and monthly returns of The 500 itself. This comprehensive approach makes it possible for market participants to gain a nuanced understanding of market behavior and the performance of individual stocks.

Exhibits 1a & 1b show the historical hypothetical levels of the index over the period for which data is available. The spikes highlight volatile market conditions where individual stocks experienced varying degrees of price movements, which often resulted in greater differences in performance. This divergence in stock returns relative to one another typically manifested as increased dispersion, making patterns and underlying risks across the market easier to observe.

It’s useful to highlight the difference between macro volatility and dispersion, particularly through the lens of historical market events. Dispersion reflects how differently individual stocks move from one another, while volatility captures the overall intensity of market movement. Correlation measures the degree to which two securities move in relation to each other. During the Black Monday crash of 1987 and the Dot-Com burst of March 2000, dispersion reached an all-time high as stock performance diverged significantly—some soared while some crashed, driven by lower correlations. In contrast, events like the Global Financial Crisis of 2008 and COVID in 2020 were marked by high volatility and high correlations, where most stocks moved in the same direction. Recognizing these patterns could provide insights into the fundamental drivers of equity market risk and return.

Exhibit 2 compares the S&P 500 30-Day Realized Dispersion Index with the DSPX, highlighting the relationship between historical and implied dispersion over time. Realized dispersion reflects the variability of individual stock returns over the past 30 days, while DSPX reflects the market’s expectation for dispersion over the next 30 days. The two series generally moved together but there were notable gaps during periods of heightened uncertainty. These divergences can offer insights into shifts in market sentiment and can help identify when the market overestimated or underestimated future stock return differences.

Conclusion: A Practical Addition to the Investment Analytics Toolkit

The launch of the S&P 500 Realized Dispersion Indices marks a notable development in the tools available to investors seeking to navigate market volatility. By providing a transparent measure of realized dispersion, these indices may help investors better understand the underlying dynamics of risk and return in the U.S. equity market.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AMD: Catalysts Align Ahead Of A 2025 Inflection Point
data.description:
The divergence observed between Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock's underperformance and its improving underlying business fundamentals this year has created a compelling opportunity. Despite a broader industry selloff driven by China-related regulatory uncertainties and tariff risks, AMD delivered a standout Q1 and robust FY25 outlook that reinforces the strength of its core business.

This widening disconnect has pushed AMD’s valuation toward levels we view as deeply discounted relative to its comparable peers. With sector-specific policy clarity likely to emerge in the second half of the year, and multiple company-specific catalysts on the horizon for AMD, the setup for a fundamental-driven rebound is increasingly clear. We believe AMD is primed for a V-shaped recovery, with current levels offering an attractive long-term entry point ahead of what could be a pivotal 12 months.

Narrowing the Competitive Gap

AMD’s data center business is emerging as a cornerstone of its long-term growth story, with the momentum behind its next-generation products helping to narrow the competitive gap in the AI compute race. While market attention has centered on the ramp-up of AMD’s Instinct AI GPU sales, strong adoption of its Zen 5 EPYC “Turin” CPUs has also surprisingly remained a bright spot. Specifically, EPYC demand remains resilient across both hyperscaler and enterprise deployments, driven by the chips’ compelling total cost of ownership (“TCO”) for general-purpose workloads and their versatility in retrofitting legacy infrastructure for AI applications.

This robust traction for AMD’s EPYC server CPUs lays a solid foundation for its next leg of data center expansion, anchored by the upcoming launch of higher-performance Instinct MI350 series GPUs in 2H25. Specifically, the MI350 series GPUs are expected to serve as a vital growth offset amid rising uncertainties in China tied to U.S. export restrictions.

Recall that the company has recently received word from the government on the restricted sale of its Instinct MI308 GPU – products previously tailored specifically for the Chinese market. The policy shift has triggered an $800 million inventory-related write-down, dealing a direct hit to AMD’s 2Q profit outlook. More significantly, the adverse development effectively eliminates AMD’s access to an estimated $1.5 billion in near-term revenue opportunities from China, underscoring the growing financial impact of escalating export controls, which remains a wildcard for the industry.

This underscores the critical importance of AMD’s upcoming upgrade cycle, led by the next-generation Instinct MI350 series GPUs. Early interest has been strong, driven by the performance and efficiency gains enabled by AMD’s latest CDNA 4 architecture – reinforcing confidence in the product’s potential to accelerate data center growth. Looking further ahead, the MI400 series – set to debut in 2026 – is expected to mark a more transformative step. Designed to better compete with Nvidia’s (NVDA) Blackwell GPUs and bundled with rack-scale server solutions via AMD’s newly acquired ZT Systems unit, the MI400 launch will deepen AMD’s penetration into the $500 billion AI accelerator market. This evolution not only unlocks incremental revenue but also enhances AMD’s data center margin profile and cash generation potential, further strengthening its valuation outlook.

Tariff Clarity Pivot

Lingering uncertainty over semiconductor sector tariffs has become a persistent overhang on valuations observed across the broader semiconductor sector. While the upcoming sector-specific tariffs are expected to be less severe than the sweeping, country-specific levies announced by President Trump in early April, the lack of visibility into final figures continues to weigh on investor sentiment.

However, much of the anticipated downside appears already priced into AMD’s current market performance. The stock fell over 30% following the Trump administration’s formal tariff announcement via the February 1 White House fact sheet, before partially rebounding in early April amid the temporary pause on reciprocal tariffs and indications of a more measured approach to semiconductor duties. Our downside scenario analysis, detailed in later sections, also supports the view that AMD’s valuation has found a defensible floor at the low $100-range.

With tariff decisions expected to crystallize within the coming months, we believe the narrative will shift from tariff “impact” to tariff “clarity.” This transition is poised to lift a key risk overhang on sector multiples and redirect investor attention toward AMD’s product-driven fundamental upside. The timing aligns well with the company’s upcoming ramp-up of next-generation Instinct MI350 and MI400 series AI GPUs, and broader data center momentum. This accordingly positions the impending sectoral tariff resolution for semiconductors as a potential accelerant to AMD’s recovery.

Strategic Upside from ZT Systems

AMD’s timely acquisition of ZT Systems, finalized in late March, has proven to be a prescient strategic move – positioning the company to deepen its reach into the growing AI infrastructure market, while also unlocking unexpected financial upside. While the acquisition included ZT’s vertically integrated U.S.-based manufacturing arm, AMD made clear from the start that its primary interest lay in integrating ZT’s server solution expertise – and not in operating its manufacturing footprint. The plan was always to divest the manufacturing component post-close.

What initially appeared to be a routine two-step transaction has now turned into a high-upside opportunity. ZT Systems’ facilities, which have historically supplied systems to hyperscalers like Microsoft, are now drawing strong interest from foreign server vendors – particularly those based in Taiwan – seeking a U.S. manufacturing base to mitigate the rising cost and demand risks posed by reciprocal and sector-specific tariffs.

AMD management recently noted “significant interest in ZT’s manufacturing business,” suggesting that a sale is likely nearing completion – and at an attractive premium. The expected divestiture not only supports AMD’s asset-light strategy, but also stands to deliver stronger-than-anticipated cash flow accretion. When paired with the core strategic goal of bundling ZT’s rack-scale server capabilities with AMD’s CPUs and GPUs, the transaction strengthens both AMD’s competitive positioning and its fundamental-driven valuation outlook. This adds yet another layer to the company’s multi-pronged tailwind ahead.

Fundamental Considerations

Updating our previous forecast for AMD’s actual 1Q25 performance and forward outlook, we expect its full year 2025 revenue to expand 30% y/y to $33.4 billion. Specifically, data center segment sales are expected to remain AMD’s key growth driver ahead, despite near-term China headwinds related to the sales ban on Instinct MI308 accelerators through 3Q. We expect data center segment sales to reaccelerate in the second half of the year, driven by the ramp-up of next-generation MI350 series GPUs alongside robust EPYC Turin uptake across enterprise and hyperscaler verticals. And despite the heightened growth base anticipated in 2H25, data center segment sales are expected to remain in strong double-digit percentage growth through FY26, as the deployment of more competitive MI400 series GPUs curated for increasing inferencing workloads start to scale.

As a result, we expect AMD’s gross margins to rebound from the $800 million cost headwind related to the MI308 export curb, and re-expand toward recent highs by year-end. Additional upside is likely, driven by a favorable mix shift toward higher-margin data center sales and growing demand for premium, higher-ASP offerings across AMD’s client, gaming, and embedded segments. These dynamics are set to amplify operating leverage gains as the company scales deployment of its next-generation product roadmap, reinforcing the strength of its margin and earnings recovery trajectory.

Valuation Considerations

We are maintaining our base case price target for AMD at $200 per share, which would represent 2x upside from current levels.

The price target is computed using the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) method, incorporating projections outlined in the preceding fundamental analysis. A WACC of 9.6% is applied, which is in line with AMD’s capital structure and risk profile. The analysis also considers a terminal value based on a 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied to projected 2034 EBITDA, which proxies terminal cash flows at steady-state. This growth assumption aligns with the upper range of long-term economic expansion prospects across AMD’s core operating regions, and reflects the company’s enduring role as a key driver of innovation and productivity in the global tech ecosystem, even at steady-state.

Final Thoughts

In our view, AMD offers a compelling long-term investment opportunity at current levels. Trading around $102 post-earnings, the stock remains below even our downside scenario price target of $105, highlighting a favorable risk-reward setup. This conservative downside case assumes a 25% haircut to AMD’s baseline FY25 growth projections, reflecting further adverse implications from looming tariff and regulatory uncertainties, while holding key valuation assumptions constant (9.6% WACC and 3.5% terminal growth). The adjustment mirrors the downward revision in economists’ 2025 GDP forecasts, from 2.2% earlier in the year to roughly 1.7% today, amid tighter financial conditions.

Yet, as outlined in our analysis, AMD’s fundamentals remain resilient and increasingly supported by a strong pipeline of catalysts – including the upcoming Instinct MI350 and MI400 GPU ramp, anticipated tariff clarity, and strategic value unlocked from the ZT Systems transaction. Together, these drivers set the stage for a sustained V-shaped recovery, underpinned by solid margin expansion, cash flow accretion, and valuation re-rating potential.
